text: expression of Eric Voegelin’s, so dominant in all variants of positivist thought. The visions of modern thinkers were always based on the claim that an absolute and positive truth about the order of the world would bring certainty to the action of man and lead the world into some kind of new realm. Like the Greek titans, they are sublimations of the image of the self-reliant shamanistic trickster. From the seventeenth century the transcendent realm had manifest itself as this-worldly truth. Hobbes and Descartes can be identified as critical key figures in this process. The notion of salvation was still driving their scientific thought. In the systems proposed by Hobbes and Descartes, secularization ultimately came to mean a possession of salvation in the here and now. As Voegelin warned (Voegelin 2004) this led to the ultimate kind of positivism, where man takes the place of God. Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 139 On that note it is certainly true that ‘absolutism’ in state organization and human knowledge is a myth, and possibly the greatest of all impossibilities. But myths have to be taken seriously in their very quality of myths. This chapter has argued that the origin of the myth of absolute knowledge and absolute statehood is religious. The idea of an absolute power over all others, that has no other source but its own power, that has no equal, no superior: this is the idea of God. It is the idea of God manifesting itself directly in the this-worldly realm, and it can be nothing other than that. There is nothing in individual sensory experience that can foster the idea of the absolute. On this point Descartes was right. The experience of an absolute power existing over and above us is transcendental, whatever the origin. The idea was transposed and applied to the level of state and self so as to deny the very reality of the transcendental experience, or so as to rule out its validity – and triumphantly so by Hobbes and Descartes. God was brought so close to rational man that he became identical with him, and so with time superfluous. When a state becomes represented as the ultimate condition of its own existence, and the human self as the ultimate condition of its own truth, then the religious realm has not been left to itself: it has been laid down, taken full possession of. Divinity has been turned into the condition of empirical being. With Voegelin’s words, this means that we have entered an age of gnosticism, an age characterized by immanentist versions of a secularized eschatology. As we shall discuss further in Chapter 8, it was this worldview that came to underpin modern political revolutions. However, to the extent that this was a process of rationalization, it happened alongside novel forms of playfulness that were not simply obliterated by reason and order. In fact, the gaming and gambling spirit, and the entire quest for adrenalin experiences which is today so dominant developed alongside scientific and political rationalization. To this aspect of liminality in the modern we can now turn. This page has been left blank intentionally Chapter 6 Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity I assert that what is serious should be treated seriously, and what is not serious should not, and that by nature god is worthy of a complete blessed seriousness, but that what is human, as we said earlier, has been devised as a certain plaything (paivgnion) of god, and that this is really the best thing about it. Every man and woman should spend life in this way, playing (paivzonta) the most beautiful games (paidiavı) (Plato, The Laws) Adventure has the gesture of the conqueror, the quick seizure of opportunity ... [yet also] the complete self-abandonment to the powers and accidents of the world, which can delight us, but in the same breath can also destroy us. (Georg Simmel, The Adventurer) Whenever we are seized with vertigo at the ceaseless shuttlings and spinnings in our mind of the thought: What is play? What is serious? we shall find the fixed, unmoving point that logic denies us, once more in the sphere of ethics. Play, we began by saying, lies outside morals. In itself it is neither good nor bad. But if we have to decide whether an action to which our will impels us is a serious duty or is licit as play, our moral conscience will at once provide the touchstone. As soon as truth and justice, compassion and forgiveness have part in our resolve to act, our anxious question loses all meaning. One drop of pity is enough to lift our doing beyond intellectual distinctions. Springing as it does from a belief in justice and divine grace, conscience, which is moral awareness, will always whelm the question that eludes and deludes us to the end, in a lasting silence. (Johan Huizinga, closing lines of Homo Ludens) I would like to start this chapter with an anecdote. In 2009 I moved with my family from the countryside to a neighbourhood in Rome. At that time, there were two small betting shops, and they were easy to spot because of the men constantly hanging around outside their doors, discussing possible outcomes of this or that horse race. Bringing my children to school in the morning morning, I would normally stop for a cappuccino at the local bar, right in front of the school. In 2009 there was one slot machine in the back room where I would skim the daily newspaper, trying to wake up properly. In 2012 there were three of those machines; a fourth, smaller betting machine was then squeezed in next to the counter in the front room. In the same period, the selection of scratch cards vastly expanded. Liminality and the Modern 142 The same thing happened in most other bars in the neighbourhood, which all of a sudden had turned into quasi-gambling houses – where coffee and cornetti also happened
